title: Building Sentences in English
subtitle: How to make affirmative, negative, and interrogative sentences correctly

content.explanation: Every English sentence needs a subject and a verb. The most common word order in English is SVO: Subject + Verb + Object. The subject tells us WHO or WHAT the sentence is about. The verb tells us the ACTION. The object tells us WHO or WHAT receives the action.
key_rules: English sentences always start with a capital letter and end with a period (.), question mark (?), or exclamation mark (!).
The subject comes BEFORE the verb in affirmative sentences.
In simple present tense, add 's' or 'es' to the verb when the subject is he, she, or it (third person singular).
Use 'do' or 'does' to form questions and negatives in the simple present.
Use 'did' to form questions and negatives in the simple past.

examples.note: Notice the 's' on 'reads' because the subject is 'she' (3rd person singular).
Notice the 'es' on 'watches' because the verb ends in 'ch' and the subject is 'he' (3rd person singular).

content.did_you_know: Word order is very important in English because the language uses little inflection. The Subject-Verb-Object sequence is obligatory in declarative sentences — unlike many other languages, you cannot freely move words around.

common_errors.incorrect: I be going to the store to buy groceries.
common_errors.correct: I am going to the store to buy groceries.

common_errors.incorrect: I goes to the store everyday.
common_errors.correct: I go to the store everyday.

content.explanation: An affirmative sentence (also called a positive sentence) states a fact or describes something. It tells us what someone DOES. In English, affirmative sentences follow the SVO order and do NOT use 'not'.
key_rules: For 'he', 'she', 'it' in simple present: add -s, -es, or -ies to the verb.
For 'I', 'you', 'we', 'they': use the base form of the verb in simple present.
In simple past, use the past form of the verb for ALL subjects (regular: +ed / irregular: special forms).
Use 'am', 'is', or 'are' to describe states, feelings, or locations (to be).
Use 'have' or 'has' to show possession (to have).

examples.note: Using the verb 'to be' to describe identity.
Using 'has' (to have) to show possession. 'She' needs 'has', not 'have'.
Base form 'study' because the subject is 'we'.
'Works' with 's' because the subject is 'he'.
Simple past: 'visited' (regular verb +ed) for all subjects.
Using 'to be' + adjective to describe a feeling.

content.did_you_know: Did you know that 'do' CAN appear in affirmative sentences for emphasis? For example: 'I did go home!' stresses that you really went. Use this only for emphasis — not in every affirmative sentence.

common_errors.incorrect: They was playing soccer last night.
common_errors.correct: They were playing soccer last night.

common_errors.incorrect: She go to the library every Tuesday.
common_errors.correct: She goes to the library every Tuesday.

common_errors.incorrect: He play guitar at the concert last night.
common_errors.correct: He played guitar at the concert last night.

content.explanation: A negative sentence tells us what someone does NOT do or what is NOT true. To make a sentence negative, we add 'not' to the sentence. The way we do this depends on the verb we are using.
key_rules: With 'to be': simply add 'not' after am/is/are.
With 'to have': add 'not' after have/has.
With other verbs in simple present: use 'do not' (don't) or 'does not' (doesn't) + base verb. NEVER add -s to the main verb.
With other verbs in simple past: use 'did not' (didn't) + base verb. NEVER use the past form of the main verb.
With modal verbs (can, will, must, etc.): add 'not' after the modal + base verb.

examples.note: With 'to be': add 'not' directly after the verb.
With other verbs: use 'do not' + base verb. 'Like' stays in base form.
'He' needs 'does not'. The main verb 'play' has NO 's'. NEVER say 'He doesn't plays'.

examples.sentence: We did not watch the movie.
examples.note: In past tense: 'did not' + base verb. 'Watch' is base form, NOT 'watched'.
With modal 'can': add 'not' after the modal.

examples.sentence: She has not finished her homework.
examples.note: With 'to have' as auxiliary (present perfect): 'has not' + past participle.

content.did_you_know: Double negatives (e.g., 'I don't know nothing') are incorrect in Standard English — two negatives cancel each other and create a positive meaning. Always use 'I don't know anything' instead.

common_errors.incorrect: I don't know nothing about cars.
common_errors.correct: I don't know anything about cars.

common_errors.incorrect: She didn't never visit the museum.
I am not can swim in the pool.
common_errors.correct: I cannot swim in the pool.

content.explanation: An interrogative sentence asks for information. In English, questions usually start with an auxiliary verb (do, does, did, am, is, are, can, will, etc.) or a question word (what, where, when, why, who, how). The word order changes: the auxiliary verb comes BEFORE the subject.

question_words.what: asks about a thing or action

key_rules: In Yes/No questions: the auxiliary verb comes BEFORE the subject.
In WH- questions: the question word comes first, then the auxiliary, then the subject.
With 'to be' questions: invert the subject and the verb (am/is/are + subject).
With other verbs in simple present: use 'Do' or 'Does' at the beginning + base verb.
With other verbs in simple past: use 'Did' at the beginning + base verb. NEVER use the past form of the main verb.
With modal verbs: put the modal at the beginning + base verb.
Short answers: Yes/No + subject + auxiliary verb (e.g., Yes, I do. / No, she isn't.)
When answering questions with 'to be' or modals, repeat the same verb in the answer.

examples.question: Do they speak French?
examples.note: 'They' uses 'Do'. The main verb 'speak' is in base form.

examples.question: Does he eat meat?
examples.note: 'He' uses 'Does'. The main verb 'eat' has NO 's'. NEVER say 'Does he eats?'.

examples.question: Did you see the film?
examples.note: 'Did' for all subjects in past. 'See' is base form, NOT 'saw'.

examples.question: Where is your house?
examples.answer_example: My house is near the park.

examples.question: What do you do?
examples.answer_example: I am a doctor. / I work in a school.

examples.question: Why does she cry?
examples.answer_example: She cries because she is sad.
examples.note: 'Does' with 'she'. 'Cry' stays in base form.

examples.question: When did they arrive?
examples.note: 'Did' + base verb 'arrive'. In the answer, we CAN use past form.

examples.question: How can I help you?
examples.answer_example: You can help me with my homework.

examples.question: Who broke the window?
examples.note: When 'Who' or 'What' is the subject, we do NOT use an auxiliary verb. The word order is like an affirmative sentence.

content.did_you_know: A declarative sentence can become a question just by raising intonation in speech (e.g., 'The book is on the table?') — but this expresses surprise, not a neutral question. For neutral questions, always use proper inversion with an auxiliary verb.

common_errors.incorrect: Does he has a new car?
common_errors.correct: Does he have a new car?

common_errors.incorrect: He no likes pineapple on his pizza.
common_errors.correct: He doesn't like pineapple on his pizza.

content.explanation: Here are the most common mistakes beginners make when building sentences in English. Pay attention to these!

mistakes.explanation: 'He' is third person singular, so use 'doesn't', not 'don't'.
After 'doesn't' or 'does', the main verb must be in BASE form. Never add -s.
After 'Did', the main verb must be in BASE form. Never use past tense with 'did'.

mistakes.correct: I don't agree. / I do not agree.
mistakes.explanation: 'Agree' is an action verb, not an adjective. Use 'do not agree', not 'am not agree'.
Questions need an auxiliary verb (do/does/did) before the subject.
In questions with 'to be', the verb comes BEFORE the subject: 'is she', not 'she is'.
Use 'do not' (don't) + base verb to make negatives. 'No' is not used this way.

mistakes.correct: Are you happy? Yes, I am.
mistakes.explanation: In short answers with 'to be', we do NOT contract 'I am' at the end of a sentence. Say 'Yes, I am.', not 'Yes, I'm.'
'Can't' is already negative. Adding 'no' creates a double negative. Use 'any': He can't play any instruments.

mistakes.incorrect: It are important to wash your hands regularly.
mistakes.correct: It is important to wash your hands regularly.
mistakes.explanation: 'It' is singular — use 'is', not 'are'. Always match the verb to the subject.

mistakes.incorrect: The results of the experiments was inconclusive.
mistakes.correct: The results of the experiments were inconclusive.
mistakes.explanation: The subject is 'results' (plural). The verb must be 'were', not 'was'.

mistakes.correct: I was eating an apple because I was hungry.
mistakes.explanation: This is a sentence fragment — it has no subject or main verb. Every sentence needs both.

mistakes.incorrect: I went to the store I bought groceries.
mistakes.correct: I went to the store, and I bought groceries.
mistakes.explanation: Two independent clauses cannot be written together with no connector. Use a comma + 'and'.

content.instructions: Complete the exercises below. Choose the correct answer or write the correct sentence.

exercises.question: I be going to the store to buy groceries.
exercises.correct_answer: I am going to the store to buy groceries.
acceptable_answers: I am going to the store to buy groceries.
exercises.explanation: The verb 'to be' is missing. Use 'am': I AM going to the store.

exercises.question: She very tired after the long day at work.
exercises.correct_answer: She is very tired after the long day at work.
acceptable_answers: She is very tired after the long day at work.
exercises.explanation: 'She very tired' is a fragment — the verb 'to be' is missing. Add 'is': She IS very tired.

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the double negative.
exercises.question: I don't know nothing about cars.
exercises.correct_answer: I don't know anything about cars.
acceptable_answers: I don't know anything about cars.
exercises.explanation: 'Don't' is already negative. Use 'anything' instead of 'nothing': I don't know anything.

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the double negative.
exercises.question: She didn't never visit the museum.
exercises.explanation: 'Didn't' + 'never' = double negative. Use only one: She never visited the museum.

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the auxiliary verb mistake.
exercises.question: How you is doing today?
exercises.explanation: 'You' uses 'are', not 'is'. The correct question form is: How ARE you doing?

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the auxiliary verb mistake.
exercises.question: She don't know the answer to the question.
exercises.correct_answer: She doesn't know the answer to the question.
acceptable_answers: She doesn't know the answer to the question.
exercises.explanation: 'She' is singular — use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. She DOESN'T know the answer.

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the subject-verb agreement mistake.
exercises.question: The dogs runs quickly to the park.
exercises.correct_answer: The dogs run quickly to the park.
acceptable_answers: The dogs run quickly to the park.
exercises.explanation: 'Dogs' is plural — the verb must be 'run' (no -s). Add -s only for he/she/it.

exercises.instruction: Find and correct the subject-verb agreement mistake.
exercises.question: She go to the library every Tuesday.
exercises.correct_answer: She goes to the library every Tuesday.
acceptable_answers: She goes to the library every Tuesday.
exercises.explanation: 'She' is 3rd person singular — add -s to the verb: She GOES to the library.

exercises.instruction: This sentence is a fragment. Rewrite it as a complete sentence.
exercises.question: She late for meeting.
exercises.correct_answer: She was late for the meeting.
acceptable_answers: She was late for the meeting.
exercises.explanation: 'She late' has no verb. Add 'was': She WAS late for the meeting.

exercises.instruction: This is a run-on sentence. Rewrite it correctly using 'and', 'so', or 'because'.
exercises.question: He failed the test he didn't study enough.
exercises.correct_answer: He failed the test because he didn't study enough.
acceptable_answers: He failed the test because he didn't study enough.
He failed the test, and he didn't study enough.
He didn't study enough, so he failed the test.
exercises.explanation: Two clauses need a conjunction or punctuation. Use 'because', 'so', or 'and' with a comma.

feedback_messages.excellent: Excellent! You scored {score}/10. Great understanding of English sentence structure!
feedback_messages.good: Good job! You scored {score}/10. Review the sections you got wrong.
feedback_messages.needs_practice: Keep practicing! You scored {score}/10. Review the lesson carefully and try again.

metadata.topic: Sentence Construction: Affirmative, Negative, and Interrogative
